narrative: I'm sorry to share that our admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 41% chance you'll face challenges there, leading to the rejection of your law school application. Your profile shows strong early promise with an eighth-decile ranking in your first-year school grades, placing you well above average peers, alongside a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 that's just a touch below typical applicants. However, your third-year decile of fifth place aligned closely with averages, marking it as the biggest factor nudging the prediction toward potential bar struggles. Close behind were your first-year decile and lower-middle family income bracket, both contributing somewhat to concerns about long-term success. Your Asian racial background and notably lower LSAT score of 21—far below the usual 36.7—also played supporting roles in tipping the scales toward caution, followed by your slightly below-average undergrad GPA. On a brighter note, being male provided a small positive lift compared to trends in our data, while your full-time study plans had minimal sway. Overall, these elements combined to outweigh your strengths, with third-year performance leading the influences, then early grades and income, racial background, LSAT, GPA, gender, and study status rounding out the order. It's clear your early academic edge faded a bit later on, differing from students who maintain top deciles throughout and pair them with stronger test scores. To boost your bar exam odds moving forward, focus on rebuilding momentum in advanced coursework to lift future deciles, as that's the top lever here. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could dramatically shift perceptions, given its pull in your case. Strengthening financial support or scholarships might ease related pressures, and targeted bar prep programs have helped many in similar profiles succeed. You're already ahead in key areas—keep building on that resilience, and reapply after these tweaks for a stronger shot.
